summary: Any valid result contains N output bits, giving an output-size lower bound of Omega(N), and the diagonal reference writes exactly one certified-different bit per input row.

optimality.argument: A solution must materialize a binary string of exactly N characters, which requires writing N output positions. The reference reads one diagonal position and writes one complemented character for each of the N rows.

replacement_checks.evidence: The required return value itself has length N, so no algorithm can produce it in o(N) time under the ordinary explicit-string model.
The accepted reference performs one constant-time diagonal read and one output write per row, then joins exactly N characters.

Authored cases cover both one-bit inputs, homogeneous and mixed diagonals, multiple valid answers, and the maximum legal length; the semantic validator enforces binary length and absence rather than one exact string.
